stories.headline: Federal judge dismisses Trump administration lawsuit against Colorado and Denver over immigration sanctuary laws
stories.summary:
A federal judge on Tuesday dismissed the Trump administration's lawsuit seeking to overturn so-called sanctuary laws enacted by Denver and the state of Colorado that limit local cooperation with federal immigration enforcement. U.S. District Court Judge Gordon P. Gallagher ruled in a 13-page opinion that federal authorities cannot compel state and local officials to implement federal regulatory programs, fully dismissing the case that was filed in May 2025.

Named defendants in the suit included Gov. Jared Polis, Attorney General Phil Weiser, Denver Mayor Mike Johnston, the City of Denver and the State of Colorado. Weiser called the lawsuit a direct attack on Colorado's sovereignty and said the ruling affirms the 10th Amendment protections that prevent the federal government from forcing states to use their resources for civil immigration enforcement.

Mayor Johnston said Denver would continue to prioritize safe communities and accountable government, while Polis spokesman Eric Maruyama reiterated that Colorado is not a sanctuary state and called the lawsuit baseless. The ruling is a significant victory for Colorado officials who have steadily tightened restrictions on ICE cooperation in recent years, and lawmakers are currently considering additional measures to further limit that cooperation.

stories.headline: U.S. Supreme Court strikes down Colorado's conversion therapy ban for minors on First Amendment grounds
stories.summary:
The U.S. Supreme Court on Tuesday struck down Colorado's 2019 law banning conversion therapy for minors, ruling 8-1 that the statute violates the First Amendment's protections on free speech. Justice Neil Gorsuch, a former Tenth Circuit judge based in Denver, wrote the majority opinion, holding that the law impermissibly regulated the content of a talk therapist's speech.

The case was brought by Colorado Springs Christian counselor Kaley Chiles, who argued through attorneys at the Alliance Defending Freedom that the law prevented her from having consensual conversations with clients who sought guidance reconciling their sexual orientation or gender identity with their religious values. Chiles does not use aversion techniques such as shock treatment, and her attorneys emphasized that patients sought her out voluntarily.

Justice Elena Kagan wrote a concurring opinion noting that because the state had suppressed one side of a debate while aiding the other, the constitutional question was straightforward. Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson was the lone dissenter. The ruling has broad implications for more than 20 other states with similar laws restricting conversion therapy for children.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers advance bill to shrink RTD board, raise member pay amid ongoing ridership concerns
stories.summary:
Colorado legislators introduced Senate Bill 150 on Monday, proposing to reduce the Regional Transportation District's governing board from 15 to nine members, raise board member salaries from $12,000 to $36,000 annually, and quadruple the number of petition signatures a candidate must collect to appear on the ballot. The bill would also have two members appointed by the governor and two selected by the Denver Regional Council of Governments, leaving only five seats filled by direct voter election.

The bill is scheduled for a Senate Transportation and Energy Committee hearing on April 8. RTD Board member Chris Nicholson said he was not opposed to downsizing the board but raised concerns about nearly half of members being unelected officials and about greatly enlarged district sizes that would make constituent outreach impractical.

Nicholson also warned that requiring 1,000 petition signatures instead of 250 could effectively price out first-time or lower-income candidates, noting that all four bill sponsors reached their seats through vacancy appointments rather than petition drives. The RTD serves approximately three million residents across eight counties in the Denver metro area.

stories.headline: Broncos trade first-round pick to Dolphins for wide receiver Jaylen Waddle
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have agreed to trade the No. 30 overall pick in the 2026 NFL Draft, along with third- and fourth-round selections, to the Miami Dolphins in exchange for wide receiver Jaylen Waddle and a fourth-round pick, according to ESPN's Adam Schefter. The deal gives the Broncos a proven pass-catcher to line up alongside quarterback Bo Nix heading into the 2026 season.

Head coach Sean Payton, speaking Tuesday at the NFL's annual spring meetings in Phoenix, called Waddle's off-field character evaluations a perfect score, saying everything checked out at the highest level. Payton acknowledged the external criticism that Denver had not been aggressive enough this offseason but argued the team had already built its core through 10 contract extensions over the previous 18 months.

Nix is entering his third professional season on a cap charge just above $5 million, giving Denver significant financial flexibility to build around him. Payton said the team's philosophy has been to retain its own players rather than rely on outside free agents, and the Broncos currently rank seventh in active cap spending despite sitting in the middle of the pack in available cap space.

stories.headline: Denver Summit FC acquires Santa Fe Yards land for $200 million women's soccer stadium
stories.summary:
Denver Summit FC announced it has completed the acquisition of land at Santa Fe Yards, a 14-acre site on the former Gates Rubber Company property at Broadway and Interstate 25, for the development of Colorado's first stadium and entertainment district built specifically for women's professional sports. The project is a partnership with the City of Denver and global design firm Populous, with an anticipated opening in spring 2028.

The city committed up to $70 million toward the project, including a previously approved $50 million for land acquisition and site preparation. The team has pledged approximately $200 million to construct the 14,500-seat venue, which is designed to host 40 to 50 ticketed events and up to 250 less-formal events per year, with mixed-use development including housing, restaurants and transit connections planned for the surrounding area.

The announcement follows the club's inaugural NWSL home match last weekend at Empower Field, which drew more than 63,000 fans and broke the U.S. attendance record for a women's professional sporting event. Mayor Mike Johnston praised the community effort behind the project, and city officials are also pursuing $25 million in federal grant funds for a pedestrian bridge connecting the stadium to the I-25 and Broadway light rail station.

stories.headline: Broncos new stadium timeline takes shape as owner Greg Penner addresses neighborhood considerations
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos owner and CEO Greg Penner provided new details Tuesday about the team's plans to build a new stadium at Burnham Yard, offering insight into the timeline and the balancing act between the franchise's ambitions and the desires of surrounding neighborhoods. The team has been pushing for an ambitious construction schedule.

City leaders are weighing community input as negotiations continue over the scale and design of the project. The Burnham Yard site sits in a redeveloping area of Denver, and residents near the proposed location have raised questions about traffic, density and neighborhood character.

The stadium planning comes alongside the Summit FC stadium announcement at Santa Fe Yards, making this a significant period for major sports venue development along Denver's South Broadway and I-25 corridor. Further details on the Broncos project timeline and city agreements are expected as planning advances.

stories.headline: Commerce City man arrested months after fatal racing crash left woman dead in his car during traffic stop
stories.summary:
Brandon Joseph Sargent-Zuniga, 22, was arrested Saturday on suspicion of vehicular homicide after investigators determined he was street racing on Interstate 270 on Jan. 16 when he crashed into a parked RV, injuring his passenger. Rather than stopping and calling 911, Sargent-Zuniga fled the scene, went home and switched vehicles before attempting to drive the woman to a hospital.

A Colorado State Patrol trooper stopped Sargent-Zuniga for speeding while he was en route to the hospital and found the woman dead in the passenger seat. A warrant for his arrest was issued Friday on two felony charges: vehicular homicide by reckless driving and leaving the scene of an accident involving death.

Sargent-Zuniga was being held Tuesday at the Adams County Detention Center in Brighton on $50,000 bail. He is scheduled to appear in court Wednesday for a setting hearing. The identity of the woman killed in the crash has not been publicly released.

stories.headline: Two Aurora sisters, ages 10 and 12, reported missing; last seen near Colfax and Sable
stories.summary:
Aurora police and the Colorado Bureau of Investigation issued an alert Tuesday for two sisters who went missing from their northwest Aurora home sometime between Sunday night and Monday morning. Annabelle Garcia, 12, and Auraelia Garcia, 10, were last known to be in the area of Colfax Avenue and Sable Boulevard at approximately 1:30 p.m. Tuesday.

Annabelle is described as 4 feet 9 inches tall with brown hair and brown eyes, last seen wearing black pants and a white long-sleeved shirt. Auraelia stands 5 feet 2 inches tall with brown hair and brown eyes and was last seen in a black hooded long-sleeved shirt, light-colored pajama pants and slippers. Police noted the sisters are familiar with public transit.

Law enforcement said it is concerned for the girls' safety. Anyone who sees them is asked to call 911 or the Aurora Police Department at 303-627-3100.

stories.headline: Denver City Council approves Axon license plate reader contract by single vote, replacing Flock cameras
stories.summary:
The Denver City Council on Tuesday approved a one-year, $150,000 contract with Axon to operate 50 automated license plate reading cameras across the city, passing the measure by a single vote. The approval came just hours after Flock Safety finished removing its more than 100 cameras that had operated under a previous, controversial contract.

The switch from Flock to Axon has been debated for weeks, with critics raising concerns about surveillance, data privacy and the concentration of law enforcement technology in the hands of a single vendor. Axon, best known for manufacturing police body cameras and Tasers, will now take over the city's plate-reader infrastructure.

The license plate reader program has been a persistent flashpoint on the council, reflecting broader tensions in Denver over the appropriate scope of automated surveillance in public spaces. The narrow margin of Tuesday's vote underscores the continued division among council members on the issue.

stories.headline: Sheridan school district cancels classes through end of week as teachers begin strike Wednesday
stories.summary:
The Sheridan school district, located in the Denver metro area, announced it is canceling school for the remainder of the week after teachers voted to go on strike beginning Wednesday. The closure affects students across the small district, which sits between Denver and Englewood.

Details on the specific contract disputes driving the strike were not immediately available, but teacher strikes in Colorado have typically centered on pay, benefits and working conditions. Sheridan is one of the state's smaller districts and serves a predominantly low-income student population.

The work stoppage adds to ongoing labor tensions in Colorado public education. Parents have been notified to make alternative arrangements for childcare and instruction through at least the end of the week.

stories.headline: Douglas County unanimously approves $65 million Zebulon Regional Sports Complex with 2028 target
stories.summary:
Douglas County commissioners voted unanimously Tuesday to approve four contracts covering the design, construction, financing and long-term operation of the Zebulon Regional Sports Complex, a $65 million, 46-acre facility targeting a December 2028 completion date. The county awarded the primary contract, capped at $65 million, to KT Development, and a separate $12 million infrastructure contract to SR Construction LLC.

Preliminary designs for the complex include multiple indoor hockey rinks, eight full-size multi-sport basketball courts, a 160,000-square-foot covered indoor turf dome, a 15,000-square-foot fitness center and outdoor baseball and soccer fields. Youth sports advocates testified at the hearing that current facilities force families to travel outside the county for training and competition, and that no public baseball field has been built in Douglas County since 2004.

The site sits on a former explosives plant that closed in 1989. State environmental regulators confirmed in 2022 that no further remediation was required after a small amount of contaminated soil was removed, and there are no current land-use restrictions on the parcel. Construction is scheduled to begin in October.

stories.headline: Mountain snow advisory issued for northern Colorado high country; Denver to see rain and cooler temperatures mid-week
stories.summary:
A winter weather advisory is in effect from 6 p.m. Tuesday through 9 p.m. Wednesday for northern Colorado mountains above 9,000 feet, covering portions of Boulder, Clear Creek, Gilpin, Grand, Jackson, Larimer and Summit counties. The National Weather Service forecasts snow accumulations of 2 to 10 inches at advisory elevations, with some of the highest summits potentially receiving up to a foot or more, and wind gusts up to 50 mph.

No snow is expected in the Denver metro area, but residents can expect mostly cloudy skies, light rain showers Tuesday evening and more widespread rain on Wednesday, with daytime highs in the upper 50s and low 60s. Forecasters warned of slippery mountain passes and difficult travel conditions during both the Tuesday evening and Wednesday morning commutes.

The storm arrives as Colorado emerges from one of the warmest and driest winters on record, with every month from October through March setting temperature records and the state facing historically low snowpack. A brief warm-up is expected Thursday before another system brings additional mountain snow and metro rain chances Friday.

joke.joke: Before you criticize a man, walk a mile in his shoes. That way, when you do criticize him, you'll be a mile away and have his shoes.

topStory.headline: Pentagon Chief Overrules Army, Clears Helicopter Crews Who Hovered Near Kid Rock's Tennessee Home
topStory.summary:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth on Tuesday lifted the suspension of two U.S. Army Apache helicopter crews who flew close to musician Kid Rock's Nashville-area home during a training mission on March 28, declaring on social media that there would be "no punishment, no investigation" and telling the crews to "carry on, patriots." The announcement came within hours of the Army confirming it had suspended the aircrews pending a review of whether they had complied with FAA regulations, aviation safety protocols, and required approval procedures. The helicopters, AH-64 Apaches from the 101st Combat Aviation Brigade at Fort Campbell, Kentucky, were filmed hovering alongside Kid Rock's swimming pool while the entertainer clapped, saluted, and raised his fist.

Kid Rock, whose legal name is Robert Ritchie, posted two videos of the encounter on social media Saturday, where they have been viewed more than 10 million times. The posts included the caption "God Bless America and all those who have made the ultimate sacrifice to defend her," along with profanity-laced criticism of California Governor Gavin Newsom, a Democrat and frequent critic of President Donald Trump. Speaking to local television station WKRN on Monday, Kid Rock said it is not unusual for helicopters from Fort Campbell to fly near his property, adding that he had previously told pilots they were welcome to pass by his home, which he calls the Southern White House and has built to resemble the White House.

President Trump, asked about the suspensions Tuesday at an executive order signing ceremony, said the crews "probably shouldn't have been doing it" and that "you're not supposed to be playing games," but stopped short of calling for discipline. He added, in a lighter tone, "They like Kid Rock. I like Kid Rock. Maybe they were trying to defend him, I don't know." Army spokesman Maj. Jonathon Bless said the training flight over the Nashville area was entirely coincidental with a "No Kings" protest against Trump taking place downtown that same day.

The episode has drawn attention to the chain of command between the Pentagon and the Army, with Hegseth's public intervention effectively ending a formal military review before it could conclude. The Army had stated Monday that aviators "must adhere to strict safety standards, professionalism, and established flight regulations" and that appropriate action would be taken if violations were found. As of Tuesday evening, the Army had not responded publicly to Hegseth's directive or clarified the status of its earlier administrative review.
